HeyWhatsThat

12:15 p.m.

12:16 p.m.
There’s a tab at the top called “New panorama”. You can use that to set up your starting point. It you have a node on a pole make sure to enter the height above ground into the tool.
cod3doomy
There’s a tab at the top called “New panorama”. You can use that to set up your starting point. It you have a node on a pole make sure to enter the height above ground into the tool.

Uncle Lit 2025-02-25 1:11 p.m.
there is a default key in T-Deck firmware, let me find it. Everyone on the UK side is using that, we should use the same key for public channel 0 around the world
1:12 p.m.
no, not default in t-deck, I had to enter it
1:15 p.m.
Forwarded
For the primary channel use the following key: izOH6cXN6mrJ5e26oRXNcg== The third character is O and not zero. This is where I got hung up with my t-decks
Originally sent: 2025-02-25 12:05 a.m.
1
1:16 p.m.
that is from our own @cod3doomy!
Uncle Lit 2025-02-25 3:23 p.m.
I just checked, this key above izOH6cXN6mrJ5e26oRXNcg== is the key used on the phone client. so if you type this key into a T-Deck you can talk between a BLE companion and a T-Deck on the public channel. You don't need to do anything if you are already running the phone client.
1
cod3doomy 2025-02-25 3:46 p.m.
I verified this with my two t-decks as well.

Birch 2025-03-01 12:43 p.m.
@Uncle Lit if @N0EJ is unable to switch frequencies do you think we should switch PNW over to 915 or is it worth forging ahead with new radios at 910.525?
Birch
@Uncle Lit if @N0EJ is unable to switch frequencies do you think we should switch PNW over to 915 or is it worth forging ahead with new radios at 910.525?
Uncle Lit 2025-03-01 1:01 p.m.
The majority of the US users are using 910.525 so far. MeshCore is new so it seems it is easy to change frequency. The best part with MeshCore you can choose to use any of the legal frequencies in the range, unlike Meshtastic. The tradeoff is obviously between freedom to choose for your community or use a common frequency to communicate with others.
cod3doomy 2025-03-01 1:02 p.m.
Cool thing about the t-deck is you can set up multiple networks at different frequencies and switch between them on the fly
3

Uncle Lit 2025-03-01 1:07 p.m.
we have the frequency on the FAQ, but it is not set in stone. There will likely be a feature where when you install the app, it will have some default frequency/BW/SF numbers for major regions and countries at setup. It is just not the highest priority for the dev at this time. https://github.com/ripplebiz/MeshCore/wiki/FAQ#q-what-frequencies-are-supported-by-meshcore

cod3doomy 2025-03-14 12:13 a.m.
I set up a repeating room server (roompeater, as some call it) so that when I’m testing out the range by driving around I can send test messages to the room and check for a delivery or failure. It’s been pretty helpful in mapping out my system’s range and antenna testing.
1
12:14 a.m.
You could also do the same with two clients and one repeater node. Just keep one of the client nodes near the repeater and talk to it through the repeater.
cod3doomy
I set up a repeating room server (roompeater, as some call it) so that when I’m testing out the range by driving around I can send test messages to the room and check for a delivery or failure. It’s been pretty helpful in mapping out my system’s range and antenna testing.

XIAO nRF52840 & Wio-SX1262 Kit for Meshtastic
This kit combines the XIAO nRF52840 and Wio-SX1262, supporting BLE5.0 and LoRa (862-930MHz), and operates with low power consumption. It can be applied in various fields such as Meshtastic, sensor networks, environmental monitoring, asset tracking, education, and innovation.

supernovasky 2025-05-23 9:46 p.m.
if you haven't made a case for one of these nodes before and you have the means, one thing people keep running into is one of the more common filament types likes to melt in heat or the sun, such as when people leave a node in a hot car—I can't quote the types that don't do this but accounting for that (unless you already did) will give your design a leg up in the durability dept
warms 2025-05-23 9:47 p.m.
yeah for production I want to do PETG which won't have that issue

supernovasky 2025-05-23 10:28 p.m.
Yeah I am doing it this way just to combine use cases because another one is needing 12v for ham bands on low-power radios where I might need to borrow some of that juice in a grid-down situation, for example
10:28 p.m.

2:52 a.m.

Uncle Lit 2025-05-24 2:53 a.m.

Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-05-24 2:53 a.m.
also it's not a violation of gpl to reimplement an allegedly open protocol in a closed source application
2:54 a.m.
copying and pasting the GPL'd code verbatim, maybe, but having looked at meshcore's codebase, I can tell you for a fact that it's not meshtastic code
2:56 a.m.
this is typical capitalist behavior, allergic to competition and will do any possible fucking thing to destroy it
Uncle Lit 2025-05-24 2:56 a.m.
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-05-24 2:57 a.m.
also what the fuck is he talking about, the CLA that meshtastic enforces (which requires all developers to sign the rights to their contributed code over to the organization known as meshtastic) puts all the cards in their hands to relicense at any time
2:59 a.m.
that is explicitly the reason why CLA's like that are bad, the only thing protecting a GPL'd codebase from being relicensed by its maintainers is the fact that usually many developers have code that has been contributed to the project, and usually retain their copyright to it when not forced to sign crap like a CLA
Uncle Lit 2025-05-24 2:59 a.m.
Ikr
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-05-24 3:00 a.m.
so if someone involved in the project tries to relicense the devs code that they hold copyright on, the dev can be like "I do not approve of this relicensing and require that all code that I hold copyright permissions on be removed before such an action is taken"
3:01 a.m.
this is typical capitalist clown behavior, he's threatened by competition and is doing whatever possible (including lies, libel, and threats) to get rid of it
3:02 a.m.
ironically, getting this shit loaded by default onto a bunch of lora devices at the factory is either going to kill the lora industry by destroying peoples trust in its ability to do what it claims, or destroy meshtastic for the same reason
3:04 a.m.
to a certain extent, I have to wonder if destroying trust in lora (while making bank) is the intended goal of the meshtastic project, but I'll try not to cut myself on Hanlon's razor on the way out (edited)
3:06 a.m.
the overt animosity demonstrated towards their own userbase that many of the devs on that project have is part of the reason I say that

Pete 2025-06-03 7:18 p.m.
I am, uh, new to all this and don't know the people involved, want to keep up but probably rather not. HOWEVER, can somebody answer this? Is it true that: (1) MQTT is the technology that lets you bridge comms thru the Internet in addition to wireless, and (2) Meshtastic has chosen to implement MQTT, Meshcore has chosen not to? And if all that is correct, is there a point being made that any perception that Meshtastic is more capable than Meshcore is skewed, because people are not recognizing that Internet connectivity is influencing the results? Do I have the gist of this...disagreement...right?
1
1
7:20 p.m.
For myself: I'm not all that interested in the drama for the drama sake, but I AM interested in better understanding how to use the technology, so I'd like to understand the general shape of whatever conflict there is. I appreciate what Meshtastic has done as a proof-of-concept, but I'm interested in emergency comms that are reliable and permit a reasonable level of privacy via encryption and lack of excess repeats. Meshcore seems really promising, and vastly more capable than Meshtastic in that regard. I'm happy to be part of communities working to get enough saturation of Meshcore in Portland that it can be reliable and enduring, and regional connectivity would be super cool too.
3
1
1
Pete
I am, uh, new to all this and don't know the people involved, want to keep up but probably rather not. HOWEVER, can somebody answer this? Is it true that: (1) MQTT is the technology that lets you bridge comms thru the Internet in addition to wireless, and (2) Meshtastic has chosen to implement MQTT, Meshcore has chosen not to? And if all that is correct, is there a point being made that any perception that Meshtastic is more capable than Meshcore is skewed, because people are not recognizing that Internet connectivity is influencing the results? Do I have the gist of this...disagreement...right?
Uncle Lit 2025-06-03 7:28 p.m.
Yes, Meshtastic supports MQTT, MeshCore does not (edited)
7:30 p.m.
Not supporting mqtt is by choice because MeshCore’s focus is reliable offline messaging. It seems that isn’t Meshtastic’s primary focus.
1
7:32 p.m.
On Meshtastic, you often see a lot of nodes on your contact list, you get to find out how much battery% is left on, but you can’t make any conversation with them.
1
Pete 2025-06-03 7:35 p.m.
Yep. I was initially excited by Meshtastic, but Meshcore launched about two weeks later, and it was immediately apparent that it has actual network design baked in. Meshtastic really just seems like a useful proof-of-concept that got enthusiastically adopted, and now needs to be enthusiastically left behind in favor of a protocol that's designed to actually work in the real world
7:39 p.m.
Thanks for the elaboration. About to climb my roof and install a 5.8 dBi!
2
Pete
For myself: I'm not all that interested in the drama for the drama sake, but I AM interested in better understanding how to use the technology, so I'd like to understand the general shape of whatever conflict there is. I appreciate what Meshtastic has done as a proof-of-concept, but I'm interested in emergency comms that are reliable and permit a reasonable level of privacy via encryption and lack of excess repeats. Meshcore seems really promising, and vastly more capable than Meshtastic in that regard. I'm happy to be part of communities working to get enough saturation of Meshcore in Portland that it can be reliable and enduring, and regional connectivity would be super cool too.
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-06-03 7:42 p.m.
the primary conflict is that real world testing has shown that meshtastic has basically never been what you could consider "reliable" in any deployment that I've heard of (with over a year of time to do so), while meshcore has been deployed in many different places, and has shown much better reliability than meshtastic in almost every case, especially for direct messages (in just a few months)
3
Uncle Lit 2025-06-03 7:42 p.m.
In Meshtastic every node by default repeats. As your local mesh scales up with more nodes, they are a lot of packets flying every which way every tube one node sends something out. Usually are the telemetry packets from all of them, drowning your text messages that you care
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-06-03 7:44 p.m.
ultimately there's reasons rooted in the way that the protocols are different from one another that can easily account for the reliability difference
7:45 p.m.
but also, in order for either solution to have a good chance of working well, you need to place repeaters in good spots with line of sight to each other (edited)
1
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-06-03 7:53 p.m.
one of the things that has been immensely frustrating to me is that there seems to be many people who are "bought in" to meshtastic and are unwilling to even entertain listening to facts and reason about how and why meshcore is a better transport layer protocol implementation than meshtastic (edited)
1
Pete 2025-06-03 8:06 p.m.
Yeah, sounds like we're all saying the same thing. Meshtastic seems to have the advantage of giving the appearance of a working network with very little effort, which is a very different thing than providing an actually useful network.
4

8:20 p.m.

8:23 p.m.

Uncle Lit
In Meshtastic every node by default repeats. As your local mesh scales up with more nodes, they are a lot of packets flying every which way every tube one node sends something out. Usually are the telemetry packets from all of them, drowning your text messages that you care
Pete 2025-06-04 12:44 a.m.
One consequence of this, that I think is underappreciated: If there ever was a catastrophic event (earthquake, wildfire etc) that knocks out electric or Internet or cell towers etc, imagine how the network would look if everybody who fooled around with a device in the last few years took one out of the closet, dusted it off, and set it up. Meshtastic, I think, would be totally useless. Meshcore might or might not suffer a bit at first, but it would be pretty obvious that oversaturation of repeaters can cause problems, and lots of clients is not such a big deal. It would be relatively easy for people to start taking repeaters offline and experiment in real time, without sacrificing their own connectivity via client nodes. (edited)
3
Pete
One consequence of this, that I think is underappreciated: If there ever was a catastrophic event (earthquake, wildfire etc) that knocks out electric or Internet or cell towers etc, imagine how the network would look if everybody who fooled around with a device in the last few years took one out of the closet, dusted it off, and set it up. Meshtastic, I think, would be totally useless. Meshcore might or might not suffer a bit at first, but it would be pretty obvious that oversaturation of repeaters can cause problems, and lots of clients is not such a big deal. It would be relatively easy for people to start taking repeaters offline and experiment in real time, without sacrificing their own connectivity via client nodes. (edited)
Uncle Lit 2025-06-04 12:56 a.m.
Yes! Absolutely! The in rush of a lot of Meshtastic clients at the same time and a lot of messages sent (because emergency) will just kill any Meshtastic mesh. We will need to test that with MeshCore too. But it has a much higher chance to work than messtastic.
2
1
Uncle Lit
Yes! Absolutely! The in rush of a lot of Meshtastic clients at the same time and a lot of messages sent (because emergency) will just kill any Meshtastic mesh. We will need to test that with MeshCore too. But it has a much higher chance to work than messtastic.
Pete 2025-06-04 12:59 a.m.
I am hopeful that once we can built up sufficient (and well-laid out) saturation -- that is, once we have enough well-placed repeaters in a given area -- newcomers generally won't have much reason to set up a repeater of their own. They can just set up a node, and possibly a room server for their group of friends, unless they actually find they need a repeater.It seems like there'd be a tipping point, maybe it takes real world experimentation to figure out where that tipping point is....
1
Pete
I am hopeful that once we can built up sufficient (and well-laid out) saturation -- that is, once we have enough well-placed repeaters in a given area -- newcomers generally won't have much reason to set up a repeater of their own. They can just set up a node, and possibly a room server for their group of friends, unless they actually find they need a repeater.It seems like there'd be a tipping point, maybe it takes real world experimentation to figure out where that tipping point is....

Pete
I am hopeful that once we can built up sufficient (and well-laid out) saturation -- that is, once we have enough well-placed repeaters in a given area -- newcomers generally won't have much reason to set up a repeater of their own. They can just set up a node, and possibly a room server for their group of friends, unless they actually find they need a repeater.It seems like there'd be a tipping point, maybe it takes real world experimentation to figure out where that tipping point is....
dit dit 2025-06-04 3:56 a.m.
This is sort of common sense, but it is also important that repeaters don't move. The Meshtastic mesh changes whenever someone moves their nodes - even just a foot on a table inside their apartment/house can have a big impact. With MeshCore, a static repeater can be placed in a reliable spot for anyone to use no matter where anybody takes their companion node. (edited)
2
1
dit dit
This is sort of common sense, but it is also important that repeaters don't move. The Meshtastic mesh changes whenever someone moves their nodes - even just a foot on a table inside their apartment/house can have a big impact. With MeshCore, a static repeater can be placed in a reliable spot for anyone to use no matter where anybody takes their companion node. (edited)
Pete 2025-06-04 11:32 a.m.
Good point. I think this kinda validates my choice last night, I was gonna add my new repeater to the Internet Map, but decided I should wait till I have the antenna in its stable/optimal spot
3

Good point. I think this kinda validates my choice last night, I was gonna add my new repeater to the Internet Map, but decided I should wait till I have the antenna in its stable/optimal spot
dit dit 2025-06-04 11:44 a.m.
Good idea. If you ever do need to change where it is on the map, you can re-add the same node again and it will move.
11:44 a.m.
Which reminds me... if you flash a different type of firmware (e.g. repeater -> companion) it will change the identifier meshcore uses, so it appear to be a different node, not just a role change. Otherwise, if you upgrade the fw, change the name location or name, etc. it will persist as the same node.
3
11:46 a.m.
Normally, if you go back to the repeater mode (after switching to companion or room in between) it will appear to be a different repeater. You can't "go back" to the same ID with the standard firmware. There are ways to restore it but it requires a custom firmware before you get started (to access the private key). (edited)
1

Pete
Well, by franklin high in south tabor, in my neighbors list it has -15 SNR. (Not sure how bad that is, I don't know the radio world too well)
akrabu 𓆫 2025-06-10 12:05 a.m.
It's not great. LoRa can decode down to -20 SNR at spreading factor (SF) 12, but that means the signal is -15 below the noise level. A positive SNR is stronger than the noise level. (edited)
1
akrabu 𓆫
It's not great. LoRa can decode down to -20 SNR at spreading factor (SF) 12, but that means the signal is -15 below the noise level. A positive SNR is stronger than the noise level. (edited)
Pete 2025-06-10 12:06 a.m.
Ah, OK. So really we want positive. Everything I see is negative, just different values. Even Woodward-Repeater, which is not terribly far away (but behind at least one line of trees if not more)
12:09 a.m.

supernovasky 2025-06-10 3:44 p.m.
I think the key difference, assuming it still is one, was that the one I mentioned would reboot into BLE DFU mode and give you another chance, which you might really appreciate if your attempt fails and your node is hard to access physically. YMMV—maybe one you've mentioned also does that now; I have no idea
supernovasky 2025-06-10 3:55 p.m.
@Uncle Lit Should this bootloader (https://discord.com/channels/1343693475589263471/1343693475589263474/1375773567337238548) be mentioned in the FAQ? Uncle Lit
I think I can mention it in the faq that an alternate bootloader is available with such DFU feature. I am not sure if the FAQ should recommend using a different bootloader than the default one. I haven't used it myself so I can't comment on it yet.
supernovasky 2025-06-10 6:29 p.m.
Yeah, I'm not sure what the best approach is here, but I get the sense that people appreciate knowing this exists (I sure did)— @recrof do you have thoughts? (About how to best inform people of the improved bootloader)
supernovasky
Yeah, I'm not sure what the best approach is here, but I get the sense that people appreciate knowing this exists (I sure did)— @recrof do you have thoughts? (About how to best inform people of the improved bootloader)
recrof 2025-06-10 6:34 p.m.
best is to mention it has infinite retries on bluetooth OTA. we use it here and it works well. @che aporeps 🌮 also created same bootloader for other boards. defo recommend upgrading the bootloader from usb mass storage dfu that accepts uf2 files. else you can have a bad time(failed bootloader update = swd programming via j-link)
1
recrof
best is to mention it has infinite retries on bluetooth OTA. we use it here and it works well. @che aporeps 🌮 also created same bootloader for other boards. defo recommend upgrading the bootloader from usb mass storage dfu that accepts uf2 files. else you can have a bad time(failed bootloader update = swd programming via j-link)
supernovasky 2025-06-10 6:43 p.m.

recrof 2025-06-10 6:45 p.m.
no idea what info medium you want to use. we just pinned info about what it does and how to install it on our local HAM discord.

recrof
working mesh pro tip: 1.7.0 repeaters/room servers have default interference threshold(set int.thresh) set to noise_floor + 14, which can be hit easily in noisy areas.. I already needed to set it to 0 because of that. if your repeater all of the sudden refuses to repeat, disable the feature.
supernovasky 2025-06-13 6:03 p.m.
@recrof when this command is tried without a value, i.e. set int.thresh it just returns "unknown config: int.thresh" as the response, by the way—room server w/ repeat enabled, 1.7.0—so it isn't apparent that it even works until you commit to a specific value (I am not sure where those are documented at the moment)
awol
Is it just to avoid having to rely on the RF switch?
Cisien 2025-06-13 6:47 p.m.
i needed separate ports for the circulator/isolator (the two silver boxes attached to the ports of the g2). In this case, the function of the switch is replaced with the circulators
1
6:47 p.m.
the circulators and filter are a hard requirement for that radio site
Cisien 2025-06-13 7:00 p.m.
that's what drove the need for the mod, nothing else. Don't do this mod without a reason to, you'll ruin your G2
Cisien
that's what drove the need for the mod, nothing else. Don't do this mod without a reason to, you'll ruin your G2

Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-06-13 7:29 p.m.

7:30 p.m.
i forgot wat circulators do
7:30 p.m.
i think k5em explained it to me once
Cisien 2025-06-13 7:30 p.m.
rx goes from antenna to receiver, tx goes from transmitter to antenna, tx doesn't go to rx
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-06-13 7:30 p.m.
I see
Cisien 2025-06-13 7:30 p.m.
it's using a magnetic field to inhibit rf flow to the undesired ports
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-06-13 7:31 p.m.
are there independant antennas for tx and rx at cougar?
Cisien 2025-06-13 7:31 p.m.
no, a single antenna
7:31 p.m.
their purpose is to prevent intermodulation
7:31 p.m.
it ensures that energy received from the antenna doesn't make it into the transmitter
1
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-06-13 7:32 p.m.

7:34 p.m.

Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-06-13 7:35 p.m.

Cisien 2025-06-13 7:35 p.m.
the load on one of them turns it into an isolator
7:36 p.m.

7:39 p.m.

Cisien 2025-06-13 7:39 p.m.
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circulator
Circulator
In electrical engineering, a circulator is a passive, non-reciprocal three- or four-port device that only allows a microwave or radio-frequency (RF) signal to exit through the port directly after the one it entered. Optical circulators have similar behavior. Ports are where an external waveguide or transmission line, such as a microstrip line or...
7:40 p.m.
that would be the flow, yes

Pete 2025-06-14 1:45 p.m.
Mere mortal here, hoping for a word of wisdom from the wise radio wizards here. It seems to connect to what's been discussed here in the last couple days, but I don't know the tech well enough to follow that discussion well. In SE Portland we have a little cluster of four people in close proximity, with access to at least five locations. We have four rooftop antennae up so far, and I think it's fair to say we have a good understanding of the basics of Meshcore/LoRa. Our problem is a ridge that goes between us; we're divided two on one side, two on the other. We have put our antennae as high as practical, and are still considering knocking on a couple doors up on the hill. However, despite what the survey says, we have intermittent connectivity without an intermediate hop! The question is: If we test and determine that, for instance, a lower spread factor or more redundant coding rate improves connectivity across the ridge, is there a way to establish a link with those settings, and bridge it to the "standard" network settings? What kind of equipment would we need for a LoRa-LoRa bridge (as opposed to LoRa-ESP32)? Tagging @Woodward , @tedder , @WanderBuddy (edited)
Cisien 2025-06-14 2:31 p.m.
you're always going to have spotty or no connectivity through that ridge
2:32 p.m.
any connectivity you are getting is likely refraction, or a hop you haven't yet seen an advert for
2:32 p.m.
if you don't have good line of sight, you won't have a reliable path
Pete 2025-06-14 3:12 p.m.
Well, we've got the antennae up pretty high, and with some placements as we've moved things around we've seen good SNR numbers. I'm curious about this question regardless of whether or not it provides a solution for this particular problem, though -- is it possible to bridge one set of LoRa settings to another, to optimize a single connection for its conditions (going thru lots of trees, going extra long distance, etc.)?
3:18 p.m.
We've also been able to exchange occasional messages, and even log into room servers, but it hasn't been reliable.
awol 2025-06-14 3:49 p.m.
It’s hard to work around no line of sight, what part of SE are you in?
Pete
Well, we've got the antennae up pretty high, and with some placements as we've moved things around we've seen good SNR numbers. I'm curious about this question regardless of whether or not it provides a solution for this particular problem, though -- is it possible to bridge one set of LoRa settings to another, to optimize a single connection for its conditions (going thru lots of trees, going extra long distance, etc.)?
Cisien 2025-06-14 6:07 p.m.
it's possible with esp32 devices and UDP. You can bridge between presets with that -- that said, if you pick presets that have drastically different performance, one of them will bottleneck
Pete 2025-06-14 8:24 p.m.
Near Atkinson Elementary to near 50th and Hawthorne. I used a mapping tool at one point (wish I saved the link) and I believe it said we needed something like 30 feet on each end (height on my end is more beneficial) to clear the ridge. I don't think we've measured the height on either end, but I'd guess they're both high 20s, and more or less maxed out to our comfort level without major construction. (Me and one of the folks over there.) Messages have been relayed multiple times between my place and both of those on the other side, but it's not reliable. It's possible one of them is just a setup thing, and is actually gonna work fine once reconfigured. My repeater consistently reports positive SNR to that one. The other, it's as bad as something like -17 SNR, but has been occasionally functional. (edited)
Cisien
it's possible with esp32 devices and UDP. You can bridge between presets with that -- that said, if you pick presets that have drastically different performance, one of them will bottleneck
Pete 2025-06-14 8:25 p.m.
But not possible to bridge one set of LoRa settings to another?
Cisien 2025-06-14 8:25 p.m.
i was thinking meshtastic -- i think in meshcore it's supported experimentally with espnow
8:27 p.m.
you need two radios, each on their own preset, and communicating over espnow
Cisien
you need two radios, each on their own preset, and communicating over espnow
Pete 2025-06-14 8:30 p.m.
Cool. I figured extra radios would be necessary for something like that, but I haven't been able to wrap my head around ESP. I thought it was a way of bridging to the 2.4 GHz range, which wouldn't really help in this use case -- but maybe it's more general? Do you happen to know a good intro resource for what ESP is and/or how to use it with Meshcore?
Cisien 2025-06-14 8:30 p.m.
espnow uses 2.4ghz, but not lora 2.4 (edited)
1
8:31 p.m.
it lets two (or more?) esp32's communicate
JQ 2025-06-14 9:03 p.m.
I think your knocking on doors idea will probably yield better results
Pete

What's the advantage of ESPNOW?
1:43 p.m.

1:46 p.m.

1
Cisien 2025-06-15 4:10 p.m.
you can use it to bridge nodes and presets
4:11 p.m.
espnow is close range
Birch
What's the advantage of ESPNOW?
Woodward 2025-06-15 4:15 p.m.
It can help with packet collision in saturated meshes, by reducing the number of nodes using the Lora frequencies. Short distances from clients to repeaters makes sense to use the 2.4 spectrum, leaving the 915 for repeaters only.

Heltec WiFi LoRa 32 V3 Case dual 18650 by SeanTheITGuy
I was looking for a robust, solid, but not overly large case for a Heltec Wifi LoRa 32 V3 board that would support a decent amount of lithium battery. Taking inspiration from (but not remixed from) https://www.thingiverse.com/thing:6767924 , I designed this case from scratch. It is not a modified version of the thing linked there, but is clearly...

mattsains 2025-06-17 6:24 p.m.

Meshcore capture the flag

Hello everyone! This Sunday morning, June 22, I will hide a meshcore node somewhere near Ballard. I’ll post here when and roughly where I’ve placed it. After I send the rough location, first to find the node and send the capture information (see below) will win a $10 amazon gift card. There is a 15 minute time advantage for anyone who arrives via public transport, walking, or by bicycle - let me know if you’ve found the node without using a car and I’ll apply this advantage to your time (honour system). The game will end at 2pm
  • The node name is mattsains2. It has custom programming on it to enable this capture the flag game.
  • Sending an advert will trigger the node into sending its own zero-hop advert. It is required that you send an advert and receive an advert from the node in order to communicate with the node
  • Sending a direct (zero hop) message will cause it to reply with a hint of its location, as well as instructions on how to win the game. If you send a multi-hop message, you will not receive this hint but you will get a different reply explaining this.
  • TDeck nodes are not recommended, because the messages are too long to display due to this bug: https://github.com/ripplebiz/MeshCore/issues/238
Rules:
  • Please return the node back to exactly where you find it, so that others can participate. Stealing the node will result in seven years of bad luck.
  • Be careful not to arouse the suspicion of muggles who may want to see what you’re searching for - be discreet.
  • Direction-finding equipment is allowed but not necessary.
  • Trying to hack the node will probably just break it and ruin the fun, so don’t bother.
  • This is my own event and doesn't have anything to do with this Discord server.
  • This is the first time I've organised such an event so it's possible it will all fall apart and be disappointing, please prepare yourself for that.
 (edited)
7
mattsains

Meshcore capture the flag

Hello everyone! This Sunday morning, June 22, I will hide a meshcore node somewhere near Ballard. I’ll post here when and roughly where I’ve placed it. After I send the rough location, first to find the node and send the capture information (see below) will win a $10 amazon gift card. There is a 15 minute time advantage for anyone who arrives via public transport, walking, or by bicycle - let me know if you’ve found the node without using a car and I’ll apply this advantage to your time (honour system). The game will end at 2pm
  • The node name is mattsains2. It has custom programming on it to enable this capture the flag game.
  • Sending an advert will trigger the node into sending its own zero-hop advert. It is required that you send an advert and receive an advert from the node in order to communicate with the node
  • Sending a direct (zero hop) message will cause it to reply with a hint of its location, as well as instructions on how to win the game. If you send a multi-hop message, you will not receive this hint but you will get a different reply explaining this.
  • TDeck nodes are not recommended, because the messages are too long to display due to this bug: https://github.com/ripplebiz/MeshCore/issues/238
Rules:
  • Please return the node back to exactly where you find it, so that others can participate. Stealing the node will result in seven years of bad luck.
  • Be careful not to arouse the suspicion of muggles who may want to see what you’re searching for - be discreet.
  • Direction-finding equipment is allowed but not necessary.
  • Trying to hack the node will probably just break it and ruin the fun, so don’t bother.
  • This is my own event and doesn't have anything to do with this Discord server.
  • This is the first time I've organised such an event so it's possible it will all fall apart and be disappointing, please prepare yourself for that.
 (edited)

mattsains 2025-06-22 11:05 a.m.

Meshcore capture the flag - event ended

Hello everyone! I have hidden a meshcore node somewhere near coordinates 47.66644, -122.3708 W. First to find the node and send the capture information (see below) will win a $10 amazon gift card. There is a 15 minute time advantage for anyone who arrives via public transport, walking, or by bicycle - let me know if you’ve found the node without using a car and I’ll apply this advantage to your time (honour system). The game will end at 3pm
  • The node name is mattsains2. It has custom programming on it to enable this capture the flag game.
  • Sending an advert will trigger the node into sending its own zero-hop advert. It is required that you send an advert and receive an advert from the node in order to communicate with the node
  • Sending a zero hop (not flooded or routed) message will cause it to reply with a hint of its location, as well as instructions on how to win the game. If you send a multi-hop message, you will not receive this hint but you will get a different reply explaining this.
Rules:
  • Please return the node back to exactly where you find it, so that others can participate. Stealing the node will result in seven years of bad luck.
  • Be careful not to arouse the suspicion of muggles who may want to see what you’re searching for - be discreet.
  • Direction-finding equipment is allowed but not necessary.
  • Trying to hack the node will probably just break it and ruin the fun, so don’t bother.
  • This is my own event and doesn’t have anything to do with this Discord server.
  • This is the first time I've organised such an event so it's possible it will all fall apart and be disappointing, please prepare yourself for that.
 (edited)
4
11:11 a.m.
and feel free to leave a guest book entry in my room server, "mattsains Ballard room" while you're there

  • Sending a zero hop (not flooded or routed) message will cause it to reply with a hint of its location, as well as instructions on how to win the game. If you send a multi-hop message, you will not receive this hint but you will get a different reply explaining this.

Pete 2025-06-27 12:48 a.m.
The thing that kills me about WhatsApp, is while the e2e encryption is good, for some reason people disregard that it requires you to merge your contacts, and all that data gets shared with you know who. Signal wants to access your contacts, but (a) it doesn't require it, and (b) the non-profit that runs it has at least some credibilty around not putting that info to use against us. (I have found ways to repress WhatsApp's voracious appetite for my contacts, but it takes...work.0
12:50 a.m.

4:27 p.m.

Tinker'r K6EH
@Uncle Lit I read in another post that the Station G2 LNA is not enabled in the Meshcore Firmware.
Uncle Lit 2025-06-27 4:29 p.m.

JQ 2025-06-27 4:30 p.m.
I think you’re thinking of RX boosted gain
Tinker'r K6EH 2025-06-27 4:30 p.m.

4:30 p.m.

JQ 2025-06-27 4:31 p.m.

4:31 p.m.

4:33 p.m.
@recrof posted a firmware with it turned back on. I’ve been running that for a few weeks
4:35 p.m.

Pete 2025-07-12 6:57 p.m.
As a Portlander, I'm very interested to see what happens if you set up some Meshcore infrastructure. Followed a bit of discussion with you on the Willamette Valley channel recently, looked to me like you were maybe separated by a ridge or something from the most promising long distance repeater...so, probably some further troubleshooting or infrastructrure to address. But it feels like we're on the cusp of making a Portland-Salem connection (there have been sightings from one to the other), and I would think something where you're at would hasten that outcome.
Pete
A dozen for me. I'll just list repeaters, probably the most interesting: Pepper Hill Ravenna Sixty Nine (purse)(home) icons (rabbit) icon Station G2 WW7STR

Pete 2025-07-14 2:52 a.m.
It seems to me like receiving-but-can't-send is a common issue until you get a good position and/or an upgraded antenna. Based on my experience and what I hear from others. I don't have that issue from home much these days, where I have a good 5.8dBi antenna on the roof. But when I'm at my partner's place with a pocket heltec indoors, I often receive multiple "public channel" messages, but can't seem to get anything out.

Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-07-16 10:05 p.m.

Pete 2025-07-16 10:05 p.m.

Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-07-16 10:05 p.m.

10:05 p.m.

Pete 2025-07-16 10:06 p.m.
wow
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-07-16 10:06 p.m.
the swr on those that I have tested are very good like <1.2 across 900mhz
10:06 p.m.
and centered around 910
supernovasky 2025-07-16 10:06 p.m.
note that they come in pairs, one with 3 lobes leaves and one with 4 lobes leaves — the 3-leaf is ostensibly for tx / 4 is for rx, but I'm not sure we have established how much this matters yet
This is a matched 3 leaf Tx to 4 leaf RX set with no cable length for a nice clean, direct install with SMA connectors.
 (edited)
1
Pete 2025-07-16 10:06 p.m.
I see many antennae for 10c, is it worth getting a few different kinds? Looks like there are two of these for 900 with maybe different connectors?
supernovasky
note that they come in pairs, one with 3 lobes leaves and one with 4 lobes leaves — the 3-leaf is ostensibly for tx / 4 is for rx, but I'm not sure we have established how much this matters yet
This is a matched 3 leaf Tx to 4 leaf RX set with no cable length for a nice clean, direct install with SMA connectors.
 (edited)
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-07-16 10:06 p.m.
i am thinking that the 4 lobe ones might me RHCP??
10:07 p.m.
it's gonna matter a lot when talking between two devices with these
1
10:08 p.m.
because LHCP polarization being rx'd by a node with a LHCP antenna is equivalent to rxing a horizontally polarized signal with a vertical antenna
10:08 p.m.
you wanna invert the polarization between nodes with direct line of sight circularly polarized antennas
10:09 p.m.
ultimately, in an urban environment idk how much it matters, because there's a ton of crap for signals to bounce off of and invert polarization on their own (edited)
Pete 2025-07-16 10:09 p.m.
I see that SMA antenna connector is what the T-Deck has and the little white Heltec v3 boxes
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-07-16 10:09 p.m.
when an LHCP signal bounces off a metal/concrete wall, it becomes RHCP
10:10 p.m.
and also, these can hear and be heard by vertical and horizontal polarized nodes equally as well
1
10:10 p.m.
so you get a slight advantage over using just a vertical/horizontally polarized antenna on your node
10:10 p.m.
because vert/horiz polarized signals ALSO rotate when bouncing (edited)
supernovasky 2025-07-16 10:11 p.m.
(for those not familiar) https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circular_polarization#Handedness_conventions (edited)
SandBoxBob 2025-07-16 10:17 p.m.
I want to put one of each on a vna and throw them on the turntable at work to look at their emission pattern.
1
Dan - WI7FI [PNW]
i am thinking that the 4 lobe ones might me RHCP??
supernovasky 2025-07-16 10:19 p.m.
Okay I took out a 4-leaf and it is oriented the same way as the 3-leaf. This video gives examples of how to tell which it is: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ej26bGd4tuU&t=189s
Robert Research Radios
Is this FPV Antenna Right or Left Handed?
10:19 p.m.
It appears that the 4-leaf AND the 3-leaf are both LHCP.
10:19 p.m.

and also, these can hear and be heard by vertical and horizontal polarized nodes equally as well
_BenjaminC_ 2025-07-17 11:09 a.m.
If youre using a circularly polarized antenna, while you will receive horizontal and vertical linearly polarized signals equally, you will loose about 3dB of signal compared to a properly oriented linearly polarized antenna.

Pete 2025-07-23 12:51 a.m.
I heard something about this kind of test in Portland. I might feel bought in if there was a writeup of what the reasoning is, why these settings, etc. Actively trying to educate myself about radio settings etc, so it would be nice to feel connected to what's going on.
Pete
I heard something about this kind of test in Portland. I might feel bought in if there was a writeup of what the reasoning is, why these settings, etc. Actively trying to educate myself about radio settings etc, so it would be nice to feel connected to what's going on.
mcarper 2025-07-23 1:07 a.m.
Long story short is you can fit a narrow bandwidth frequency range into a congested band. So the most narrow bandwidth that MC supports is 62.5khz. Then you adjust your SF to make sure it's not super slow
2
1:07 a.m.
1:08 a.m.
SF7 BW62.5 gets us a good speed bump and typically better signal to noise ratio because the noise floor is lower
mcarper
Long story short is you can fit a narrow bandwidth frequency range into a congested band. So the most narrow bandwidth that MC supports is 62.5khz. Then you adjust your SF to make sure it's not super slow
Pete 2025-07-23 1:26 a.m.
Thank you! This is the video I got the most from a month or so back...I'm gonna review it now, other newbies might benefit from this one too. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LkSEeOLaKXs
The Comms Channel
Advanced Meshtastic - LoRa Configuration
1
mcarper 2025-07-23 1:40 a.m.
That's one of the best videos I've seen on what the different configurations do. One thing to note is the coding rate doesn't have to match with other radios. So on a pocket node setting it to 4/8 makes sense but for a rooftop 4/5 makes more sense unless you're right on the edge of being heard then bump it up until you have a good link to the rest of the network.
mcarper
That's one of the best videos I've seen on what the different configurations do. One thing to note is the coding rate doesn't have to match with other radios. So on a pocket node setting it to 4/8 makes sense but for a rooftop 4/5 makes more sense unless you're right on the edge of being heard then bump it up until you have a good link to the rest of the network.
Pete 2025-07-23 2:39 a.m.
Cool, I see, the proposed settings are very different from the ones in any of the MT presets discussed in that video. It has bandwidth (62.5) that's only used in the "Very Long/Slow" preset, and spreading factor (7) that's only used in the "Short/Fast" preset. Interesting that settings so far off the default would be expected to yield better results!
1
2:40 a.m.
To prepare for the test and get the most out of it, what can we do? I'm guessing the following:
  • Get around to any firmware updates I've been putting off
  • Record the Neighbours list from rooftop repeater, with most recent SNR values
  • Record SNR values from frequently contacted stationary nodes
  • Anything else?
Pete
To prepare for the test and get the most out of it, what can we do? I'm guessing the following:
  • Get around to any firmware updates I've been putting off
  • Record the Neighbours list from rooftop repeater, with most recent SNR values
  • Record SNR values from frequently contacted stationary nodes
  • Anything else?
mcarper 2025-07-23 2:55 a.m.

mcarper
You need your entire network to switch over to the new settings. So pick a day and time and have everyone switch over to the new settings

Pete

10:56 a.m.

10:56 a.m.

10:56 a.m.

Pete 2025-07-23 10:56 a.m.
I don't think the name has to 100% explain it. But, "slow/fast" does not cognitively map onto "spreading factor" and "long/short" does not cognitively map onto "bandwidth". Getting closer to something that can be grasped would be a big improvement. I don't have density and thickness are both things that make sense v-a-v spreading.
10:58 a.m.

1:12 p.m.

ded (SalishMesh) 2025-07-23 1:19 p.m.

2
mosssupply 2025-07-23 1:40 p.m.

Architect [KJ7CXC] 2025-07-23 2:08 p.m.

Uncle Lit 2025-07-23 3:37 p.m.
with all the Station G2 talks, I want to add that the Nano G2 is also very good. I think it is the best EDC device, after I printed an enclosure for it to add a 3000mAh battery. I received it on Friday. Charged it over night. Starting using it on Saturday. Today is Wednesday, 4 days later. It is still on the same charge. app says it is at 38% 3.46V. I have GPS turned off. I has a hardwired physical power switch to turn it off. also hardwired switch for power and buzzer. The Nano G2 internal antenna is just as good as a RAK with a good antenna when both are sitting on a table. The Nano G2's internal antenna is supposed to still work well when close to a body. It does seem to receive OK about a 1 mile away with the device on my body.

Yeah... I understand and I'm all for presets. @mcarper has done amazing work on this and of course that MegaComboChart.   • Australia
  • New Zealand
  • USA etc etc. And then another section for "Community Meshes" that have a GPS location or geo fence that can be visualised on a map overlay. Main use cases I can think of, are probably:
  • I just want to tinker and use this in my country
  • I want to join a community mesh, that might be using some obscure settings
  • I want to setup a private mesh on a frequency away from others For the user experience, in the app, you'd connect to a new device, and it would ask for your display name, and then ask you to pick radio settings. I'd have the UI setup where you can:
  • Pick a default suggestion for your country/region
  • or, search from a list of mesh communities, like "Puget Mesh", "SoCal Mesh" or "GizzyMesh" etc
  • or enter custom radio settings if you want something custom I think the colouring system might end up like the LongFast, MediumShort thing, where you don't quite know what they mean without looking at some table somewhere, but it's been great to get discussion rolling
5
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-07-24 3:11 a.m.
I'm not sure how I feel about region names, because I still think there's a future where each local mesh consists of a number of different presets, and the one you pick depends on your proximity to the urban core
Liam Cottle 2025-07-24 3:33 a.m.
Can call the community entries whatever you want, but if they collide with other names from other countries/regions, they'd need to be nested under some sort of group name, such as the country or region otherwise users wouldn't know what it's for or related to. Ie, SoCal Mesh, has no meaning in New Zealand, so if I saw that as an option I'd have no idea if I should select it or not. But I think the geo zoning would be a good option. Just look at the map and pick from meshes in your area.
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-07-24 3:36 a.m.
maybe something can be done with the map to show preset delineations?
Cisien
maybe consider adding a note that the SNR changes, on an SX1262 radio, the max on 250khz is roughly 7, at 62.5khz, it's around 12 or 13. The minimum SNR also goes up. you should expect connections to start getting flaky below 0, and an absolute minimum around -7.5
Pete 2025-07-24 12:02 p.m.
I wish I understood this well enough to act on it, but I don't. Maybe relevant, I imagine this document as a way to help people understand what's going on and participate in it, which is a different thing than making decisions about what will be tested.
12:03 p.m.
I don't like region names because it hard-codes something where there should be flexibility.
mcarper 2025-07-24 12:09 p.m.
11_250: -131.5 dBm 7_62.5: -127.5 dBm So the new settings in a noise free environment preforms 4dBm worse. In a RF noisy environment (the real world) it usually performs better due to the more narrow bandwidth and being able to fit between the noisy rf.
Pete
I wish I understood this well enough to act on it, but I don't. Maybe relevant, I imagine this document as a way to help people understand what's going on and participate in it, which is a different thing than making decisions about what will be tested.
Cisien 2025-07-24 12:22 p.m.
i think the takeaway is that you can't directly compare SNR between 250khz and 62.5
12:22 p.m.
the window is wider, and also, the link budget difference on SF7 vs SF11 is more, which raises the minimum
Cisien
the window is wider, and also, the link budget difference on SF7 vs SF11 is more, which raises the minimum
Pete 2025-07-24 12:26 p.m.
Oh, I see. So the info in "how can I participate" isn't accurate/useful. The numerical values of SNR under two presets is not very useful info. So, I think the best first edit is maybe to just remove that line? Is it better to encourage people to do more qualitative investigation (e.g, how reliable is my ability to communicate with what other nodes)?
Cisien 2025-07-24 12:27 p.m.
yeah, i think that's ultimately what matters

12:31 p.m.

4:42 p.m.
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leaky_feeder is used specifically for this use case
Leaky feeder
A leaky feeder is a kind of antenna used for communication in mines, tunnels, and other enclosed spaces. The commercial name radiating cable emphasizes that it is designed to radiate, unlike most cables.

ded (SalishMesh) 2025-07-24 5:35 p.m.

Dan - WI7FI [PNW]
my question was around whether or not there's a pki challenge from the webservice for uploading companion nodes to the map
Liam Cottle 2025-07-24 5:44 p.m.
The mobile app uploads internet map nodes to my server, which then forwards to recrofs map server. The node info being uploaded is signed by your companion key, which allows us to mark it as being uploaded by you. In which case when you try to delete a node, this is also signed by your key, and allowed if you were the one that uploaded it. My API server also prevents you from uploading companion nodes that are not you. (edited)
1
Liam Cottle
The mobile app uploads internet map nodes to my server, which then forwards to recrofs map server. The node info being uploaded is signed by your companion key, which allows us to mark it as being uploaded by you. In which case when you try to delete a node, this is also signed by your key, and allowed if you were the one that uploaded it. My API server also prevents you from uploading companion nodes that are not you. (edited)
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-07-24 7:31 p.m.
awesome, thanks for the clarification @Liam Cottle
7:31 p.m.

Cisien 2025-07-27 2:01 a.m.
a few things i've been experimenting with this week -- I have multi-acks enabled -- i don't know if this is helping but it doesn't seem to hurt. I also changed the tx delay to a high value, 2.0. The theory there is that it gives the local repeaters time to do their thing before the highly visible repeater stomps over everything
2:01 a.m.
and i'll be doing some work to find a better frequency for "Narrow" so it's not the same for both, we should compare notes when I get a chance to do a capture of the spectrum

a few things i've been experimenting with this week -- I have multi-acks enabled -- i don't know if this is helping but it doesn't seem to hurt. I also changed the tx delay to a high value, 2.0. Cisien 2025-07-27 3:58 p.m.
Neighbors or something else?
shrike 2025-07-27 3:59 p.m.
Neighbors but also paths like you can see in the app for public messages
Cisien 2025-07-27 4:13 p.m.
Path has a few edge cases. We only have the first two characters of the public key, so the path would be best effort, and it doesn’t include info about the companions at either end
4:15 p.m.
@alex has been working on the web app, not sure if there are plans to add this
alex 2025-07-27 5:16 p.m.
i’ve been thinking about this but the potential for collisions is pretty high. the idea for the web map is to be global eventually so it would run into collisions frequently. i had an idea to assume that the geographically closest node with the same key prefix is the next hop but even that’s not always true. additionally some repeaters do not broadcast their position or have inaccurate positions
5:18 p.m.
i could show the raw path info (first byte of key) but associating hops with specific nodes will always rely on some level of assumption (edited)
alex 2025-07-28 3:03 a.m.
added raw path info for now

4:03 p.m.

Cisien 2025-07-29 4:04 p.m.
search for "WW7STR"
4:06 p.m.
it was essentially one of the members of the HAM community with access to the tower on cougar with a spare 900mhz antenna that opened the doors. we did a couple of tests and a poll in the puget mesh discord to decide on mode, and then we got it installed

1

Uncle Lit 2025-07-29 4:13 p.m.
we got a taste of what a working mesh is like when Cisien brought a G2 up to cougar in may to test for 30 mins. A dead, disconnected mesh came alive. After the test was done, the mesh went dead again. From then on, we knew it will work and what is needed to make a working mesh.
1
Uncle Lit
we got a taste of what a working mesh is like when Cisien brought a G2 up to cougar in may to test for 30 mins. A dead, disconnected mesh came alive. After the test was done, the mesh went dead again. From then on, we knew it will work and what is needed to make a working mesh.

Uncle Lit 2025-07-29 4:30 p.m.
At home I have a good connection to cougar. At my son’s place in Seattle, it is a low floor condo with taller building surrounding it. It has no LOS to cougar or any repeater. mannkind 2025-07-29 5:09 p.m.
"The Ranch" is a very lonely node that used to be a MT node out at my parent's house before Cougar went up. IIRC, I don't think the map of Cougar's coverage reached that far. The elevation there is somewhere around 700ft, but the node is only about 15ft AGL right now. It's still on the original MeshCore settings at the moment, but I was gonna swap it when I go down there in a couple weeks. (edited)

Wavesonics 2025-08-19 11:22 p.m.
hey how do I add a repeater/room server to the internet map?
11:23 p.m.
from the remote interface that is
Wavesonics
hey how do I add a repeater/room server to the internet map?
Liam Cottle 2025-08-19 11:57 p.m.
If you're using the mobile app, you can use the 3dot menu on the contact list, select share, and then tap internet map.
Wavesonics 2025-08-20 12:00 a.m.
ah nice, that does it. I feel like it would be a nice option to also have in the Remote Management screen, like nets to the Set position menu item
12:01 a.m.
maybe this doesn't make sense, but is there a way to set my local repeater as the preferred path for all messages?
Wavesonics
ah nice, that does it. I feel like it would be a nice option to also have in the Remote Management screen, like nets to the Set position menu item
Liam Cottle 2025-08-20 12:05 a.m.
Yeah would be nice, but the upload to map functionality has to use the advert data from your contacts. So if you set position via remote admin, don't send/receive an advert, and then upload the repeater to internet map (ie from the new button you suggest), it won't actually upload with the position you just set. Having the button on the contacts list makes it more connected to the fact that you're sharing the current state of the data from your contacts list
Wavesonics 2025-08-20 12:06 a.m.
huh interesting
Liam Cottle 2025-08-20 12:06 a.m.
Adverts are signed by the node that does the advert. The internet map only accepts valid signatures
12:07 a.m.
This prevents spoofing. So you need to receive an updated advert, before uploading to the map
Wavesonics
maybe this doesn't make sense, but is there a way to set my local repeater as the preferred path for all messages?
Liam Cottle 2025-08-20 12:08 a.m.
pathing is done on a per-contact basis. the firmware stores the outbound path on the contact record
12:08 a.m.
by default, it will flood the network to find a path, then from that point onward, it will use that specific path
12:08 a.m.
if the app detects a direct message failed to deliver, it will revert to flood to find a new path
12:09 a.m.
there's no concept of "use this repeater" for all contacts. it's either "use this exact path", or "flood the network to find a path"
12:10 a.m.
you can of course set the path manually on the contact, but you can't set it for all contacts in one go
Wavesonics 2025-08-20 12:11 a.m.
okay that makes sense, so it'll just figure out that the repeater is the best path
12:11 a.m.
If a contact is available via direct, but w\ a much worse SNR, will the app choose the repeater then? (edited)
Liam Cottle 2025-08-20 12:12 a.m.
it'll use the first path that makes it there and back
12:13 a.m.
a future update might allow for selecting the better path out of all of the paths that made it there/back. but for now, it's just whatever was first
1
Wavesonics 2025-08-20 12:14 a.m.
nice, ya that'd be a cool feature

ded (SalishMesh) 2025-08-22 9:52 p.m.

9:52 p.m.
Ping pong over there
Cisien 2025-08-22 10:08 p.m.
it only transmits once, cougar hears all
Dan - WI7FI [PNW]
like this for example WW7STR/PugetMesh Cougar 83 24 7E 1F 38 F5 (7E)
Cisien 2025-08-22 10:09 p.m.
the value in parens is the repeater's prefix, 7E in cougar's case
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-08-22 10:48 p.m.
word
Cisien
it only transmits once, cougar hears all
🌲 Tree 2025-08-23 12:46 a.m.
Cougar hears only the winners of the great collision royale that follows it repeating something. Its results will never be consistent either because of the randomness in the repeat txdelay
12:47 a.m.
Longer messages in particular will be more prone to repeat collisions, and message queues that vary in length on different devices at different times also adds another layer of fun
12:54 a.m.
I can count on some repeaters to almost always lose/never appear in repeats heard on w0z, but they repeat cougar almost every time. Some repeaters just have a weaker signal and are doomed to lose
Cisien 2025-08-23 1:39 a.m.
If they are repeating cougar then there is no harm if cougar doesn’t hear them
1:39 a.m.
(Because cougar already repeated that message)
Dan - WI7FI [PNW]

A python based script to send meshore debug and packet capture data to MQTT for analysis Requires meshcore repeater to be connected to a raspberry pi, server or similar linux device able to run py...
MeshExplorer
A real-time map, chat client, and packet analysis tool for mesh networks using MeshCore and Meshtastic.

2:09 a.m.

2:10 a.m.

Panic 2025-09-01 2:10 a.m.
The HamWAN backbone operates on Part 15 rules, not Part 97 rules

2:10 a.m.

2:11 a.m.

Panic 2025-09-01 2:11 a.m.

2:12 a.m.

2:12 a.m.

Cisien 2025-09-01 2:13 a.m.

Panic
Or don't worry about it ;)
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-09-01 2:13 a.m.

Panic 2025-09-01 2:13 a.m.
The first decade of HamWAN operated under my license. I was prepared to explain away those use-cases, but nobody ever asked.
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-09-01 2:14 a.m.

2:14 a.m.
Panic 2025-09-01 2:14 a.m.

Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-09-01 2:15 a.m.

1
2:15 a.m.
xd
Panic 2025-09-01 2:17 a.m.
My philosophy is that the prohibition of encryption is a good thing to make sure the resources aren't secretly clogged up with commercial use-cases. However, the packet-switched nature of the IP network means that many people can share the resources at the same time, so you're not preventing someone from communicating by sending garbage youself.
Panic
My philosophy is that the prohibition of encryption is a good thing to make sure the resources aren't secretly clogged up with commercial use-cases. However, the packet-switched nature of the IP network means that many people can share the resources at the same time, so you're not preventing someone from communicating by sending garbage youself.
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-09-01 2:30 a.m.
i largely agree

loather

3:45 a.m.

3:46 a.m.

loather 2025-09-01 3:49 a.m.

3:50 a.m.

Panic 2025-09-01 3:51 a.m.

3:53 a.m.

3:57 a.m.

3:57 a.m.

mcarper 2025-09-05 1:22 a.m.
How big of a lifepo4 12v battery is needed to run a 4631 for an entire year? Given a 3% ch usage
Cisien 2025-09-05 1:32 a.m.
Find out
mcarper 2025-09-05 1:40 a.m.
Dig a hole with this battery buried https://batteryhookup.com/products/8s-30-4v-67ah-2-04kwh-lithium-ion Cover with this and put the 8db low profile antenna inside it https://www.homedepot.com/p/Emsco-Extra-Large-Resin-Landscape-Rock-2280-1/100598430 (edited)
8s 30.4v 67ah 2.04kWh Lithium Ion
These batteries are perfectly balanced and were only used as backup for a data center. Each of the busbars, as you can see, has a spade connector area so you can make your balance lead connection there or simply just solder your connections to that nickel spade connector part. Very easy to wire your own BMS. Each cell
Emsco Extra-Large Resin Landscape Rock 2280-1 - The Home Depot

🌲 Tree 2025-09-06 3:36 p.m.

[PNW] HerculesMulligan 2025-09-06 3:39 p.m.
@🌲 Tree "Granite Falls (" - 15 regular ASCII characters = 15 bytes "Soon" - 4 ASCII characters = 4 bytes "™" - trademark symbol, which is 3 bytes in UTF-8 ")" - 1 ASCII character = 1 byte Total: 15 + 4 + 3 + 1 = 23 bytes (edited)

Does their $9 come from the MeshCore app's In App Purchase of $7.99 for Remote Admin or the £8.00 (about $11) Ripple firmware unlock code stuff? I've seen a few comments on reddit about both, and I can see how that gets misunderstood as some kind of pay to use. I know the IAP doesn't unlock functionality and just bypasses a timer and that the Ripple ultra unlock isn't required to use, but on glance both can be misunderstood or deterrents

Uncle Lit 2025-09-23 8:02 p.m.
meshtastic: "free" but you pay royalty to meshtastic llc every time you buy the version with meshtastic preloaded, if you even get a choice to choose between preloaded with meshtastic firmware or not. For example, Rokland doesn't tell you when and how they pay meshtastic llc in their blog post about joining the meshtastic llc's "backers program". also meshtastic: you are fucking up the mesh by choosing the wrong role out of 13 different vaguely named client roles
1
🌲 Tree 2025-09-23 8:03 p.m.
meshtastic: "free" but you pay royalty to meshtastic llc every time you buy the version with meshtastic preloaded, if you even get a choice to choose between preloaded with meshtastic firmware or not.
While I'm aware that some Meshtastic SKUs pay a royalty to Meshtastic, and that does create confusion for their users, it's also possible to just not buy those variants of the product? I'm not saying Meshtastic is better, but their pricing strategy of the software is free in all forms is very appealing and very simple

they are GPL, so they go apeshit and think you are stealing if you use their code
🌲 Tree 2025-09-23 8:20 p.m.
Only if you don't comply with the GPL! If you're just doing open source stuff under the same license then there's no issue. It's the same reason all my previous employers did not permit the use of any GPL code internally, because it introduces open source compliance headaches like keeping track of all changes to it and ensuring they're made public. But if I made my fork public, then that's not any issue at all of course (edited)
8:21 p.m.
But totally agree, GPL is definitely thorny
mcarper 2025-09-23 8:27 p.m.
v2 or lgpl allows for SAAS
1
🌲 Tree 2025-09-23 8:35 p.m.
Lots of things go the opposite way these days unfortunately, like Hashicorp from MPL 2.0 to BSL 1.1 (edited)
8:35 p.m.
Sustainable open source is very challenging

10:57 p.m.

mcarper 2025-10-31 11:00 p.m.
Built in rate limits would probably be a good idea
🌲 Tree 2025-10-31 11:03 p.m.
I would like to understand more about how Scott plans to do the private mesh stuff, because it needs to be more substantial than "change a few bits"
11:03 p.m.
There needs to be some form of shared secret and signature, with enough bytes of signature that it's not trivial for someone else to simply set those bits
11:04 p.m.
If you ignore sub meshes entirely and just consider the packet data we have today, if someone sends constructed packets to Cougar, Cougar is going to repeat them. There's no checks, no rate limiting, nothing helpful.
11:05 p.m.
If you wanted to say "rate limit by source hop", then an abuser need only use a new fake path/source hops for each abusive packet
11:06 p.m.
My biggest gripe about people is that they/we tend to react to problems rather than proactively avoid them. it would be easy for someone to shut down the mesh if they really wanted to, or at least degrade its usability to the point it's nothing but frustrating
2
11:07 p.m.
At a protocol level, this needs to be addressed. It's not easy with the budget constraints/bandwidth, but it's not optional. (edited)

supernovasky 2025-10-31 11:11 p.m.
Repeaters that can be managed from nearby, should be, as well, not from several hops away. NOr should a lot of repeaters be under one or two people's control. Someone gets sick (or has a tantrum) and a chunk of your mesh is effed. No contingency planning = planning to fail.
mcarper 2025-10-31 11:11 p.m.

nullrouten 2025-11-01 10:23 p.m.

10:24 p.m.

mcarper 2025-11-01 10:30 p.m.

1
nullrouten 2025-11-01 10:31 p.m.
Yes we were out of hops going 200 miles… now we’re going 300 miles.. we can do 350’miles from Chico to Paso Robles
10:31 p.m.
But the reliable core Yuba City to Carmel
10:36 p.m.
We hit 60-70 loggers on most packets … and got 530 nodes moved to medium fast in a week…. A large part of our success is how many of our population is engaged and present. And some of us are now repeating this with MC… but it’s , so far, harder to observe ( many islands , not a lot of loggers , but we’re gettin there. ). Big gains from the first and second hills! It’s like our medium slow-MT mesh exactly 1 year ago.
1
mcarper 2025-11-01 10:38 p.m.
PNW was all clients for the most part. For us it didn't work unless you were in a core
nullrouten 2025-11-01 10:40 p.m.
We weren’t afraid of routers. Have 43. (Of 530). Anyone in an advantageous position for their area needs to route or things won’t work. We figured that out super early (the “never route!!” Meme kills meshes )… but things that route need to have altitude or a view of some sort. Lots of client base and router late now
1
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-11-01 10:41 p.m.
with MT, routers is non-advantageous positions is actively harmful to the mesh imx
nullrouten 2025-11-01 10:41 p.m.
Not if you have routers every 5-10 miles
10:41 p.m.
Let em
10:41 p.m.
They do the work
10:42 p.m.
Shut the useless clients up
mcarper 2025-11-01 10:42 p.m.
I was thinking that a client/client mute and router late only would be the best. Router being able to mute is a bad thing (edited)
10:42 p.m.
Just use more tx
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-11-01 10:43 p.m.
i think router_late makes some amount of sense but quite frankly i don't wanna think about it that hard
10:44 p.m.
having one role and no confusing "managed" flood behaviors simplifies the hell out of managed deployments
1
10:44 p.m.
it hasn't shown to be very beneficial to MT
nullrouten 2025-11-01 10:46 p.m.
Most valleys have Routers in 3 directions … makes everyone have 3 chances to send/rx packets (they just need a view of any hills ,,,, not “the one hill”…. )… redundancy fixed the mesh. We know because everyone is logging and the more routers we deployed the more of the mesh was reachable Client is fundamentally broken (black hole problem), so we worked around it. It moved airtime from clients To those “best 7%” of any 10 mile area….
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-11-01 10:55 p.m.
yeah that makes a certain amount of sense
nullrouten 2025-11-01 10:55 p.m.
We had 650 nodes happy on MS, then deployed MF and 530 moved…. (Everything works even better now)… has been humming the whole time … now 0-hop made conversations better (thank you)… because we don’t run out of hops “sometimes”. Now we hit the edges so nobody is left out. Going to be interesting as we roll MC now… thanks to lower background noise and more hops. And if you think about it… MC basically encourages repeating …. It’s doing what we did with MT a year ago (just deploy routers whenever you need them). Hops was another fun meme. Scolded for going over 3… until we got a dozen people on 7 and “oh gee we can talk now”. Now we’re easily hitting 9-11 thanks to 0hop (so I guess exceeding 3 wasn’t so horrible after all). It wasn’t without resistance … I got scolded until I had 2 - 3 routers on hills and people then discovered the mesh started working and they could chat.. then we just built out across the land . Luckily both those taboo things are gone with MC
10:59 p.m.
Sorry I’ll stop preaching, just excited to build a third mesh … and hopefully ignore some rules and guidance again, to see what happens
mcarper 2025-11-01 11:05 p.m.
We have a local MT dev so we did the deployment by the book and ended up with a broken mesh that didn't work. Yeah breaking the MT rules and you'll get something working better
nullrouten 2025-11-01 11:06 p.m.
Truths.
nullrouten
We had 650 nodes happy on MS, then deployed MF and 530 moved…. (Everything works even better now)… has been humming the whole time … now 0-hop made conversations better (thank you)… because we don’t run out of hops “sometimes”. Now we hit the edges so nobody is left out. Going to be interesting as we roll MC now… thanks to lower background noise and more hops. And if you think about it… MC basically encourages repeating …. It’s doing what we did with MT a year ago (just deploy routers whenever you need them). Hops was another fun meme. Scolded for going over 3… until we got a dozen people on 7 and “oh gee we can talk now”. Now we’re easily hitting 9-11 thanks to 0hop (so I guess exceeding 3 wasn’t so horrible after all). It wasn’t without resistance … I got scolded until I had 2 - 3 routers on hills and people then discovered the mesh started working and they could chat.. then we just built out across the land . Luckily both those taboo things are gone with MC
Wavesonics 2025-11-01 11:16 p.m.
n00b here, what is MF?
nullrouten 2025-11-01 11:16 p.m.
Medium Fast in meshtasticeese
Wavesonics 2025-11-01 11:17 p.m.
ooo
11:17 p.m.
got ya
nullrouten 2025-11-01 11:17 p.m.
Long fast 1kbps Medium slow 2kbps Medium fast 3.5kbps, or something like that (don’t quote me)… basically how fast things go… preset sets the Bandwldth, spreading factor , coding rate, etc. MC just lets you set these things manually (edited)
Wavesonics 2025-11-01 11:18 p.m.
is that mainly spreading factor?
nullrouten 2025-11-01 11:19 p.m.
Mostly because LF, MS , and MF are all 250khz, … iirc
11:19 p.m.
Long slow was 125khz, short turbo was 500 kHz. For what it’s worth, we also tested short turbo in our area and had 95% of our links still working, which blew our minds
11:20 p.m.
But we didn’t wanna leave 5% of our core areas and 10 - 20% of our fringe areas out in the cold …. so we went to medium fast instead
mcarper 2025-11-01 11:21 p.m.
I heard burning man had issues with short turbo not meshing due to traffic utilization but I could be mistaken
nullrouten 2025-11-01 11:23 p.m.
They had 3-4 networks going… I think only half of the participants flashed the event firmware, and the other half were spread among Longfast and other things…. We do have a copy of all the packets from the event. If we wanted to analyze it, someone was logging and we snarfed the database for analysis. We haven’t done much.
11:24 p.m.
But we’d have to know which network had that problem to figure out what happened I lent a bunch of radios to some friends and they said it worked great for them, but they were on I think a private channel
11:24 p.m.
That’s another factor …. they assigned private channels to dozens of camps … so maybe I should say “they had 30 meshes going at once”
mcarper 2025-11-01 11:26 p.m.
Same frequency? Did your data logging have precise time (gps) or is the data analysis going to be difficult
nullrouten 2025-11-01 11:27 p.m.
The logging would be from the perspective of one radio receiving the packet so it’s far from perfect, but timing is absolute from that radio‘s Linux host
1
11:27 p.m.
And of course, that means they couldn’t capture all the meshes, just the two or three main well-known presets and channels
1
nullrouten
The logging would be from the perspective of one radio receiving the packet so it’s far from perfect, but timing is absolute from that radio‘s Linux host
DrivenMadz 2025-11-02 12:22 a.m.

nullrouten 2025-11-02 12:23 a.m.
No idea … I can find out… I have one
DrivenMadz 2025-11-02 12:24 a.m.
That would help the "timing is absolute from that radio‘s Linux host"
12:24 a.m.
since any power cycle on RAK drops its time
Cisien 2025-11-02 12:44 a.m.
works on my g2
12:44 a.m.

https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0FKN5V4D2 modules https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0D5VKR885 cables
Originally sent: 2025-11-01 3:45 p.m.
12:45 a.m.
just need to hook it up to power and i2c
DrivenMadz
That would help the "timing is absolute from that radio‘s Linux host"
nullrouten 2025-11-02 12:50 a.m.
No, I think you misunderstood me, all the mesh radios can have any time they want …. Wildly inaccurate . the time the packet is received on the Linux host and writes it to the database …. with that timestamp.
12:52 a.m.
So any packets that don’t hit that host are just invisible … so it’s not 100% representation of reality… and of course it’s the time packets were received at one point …. not the time they were sent from around the Playa…. but from what I hear the entire area was “direct” and sometimes one hop, rarely 2… they did have 4 routers barely half a mile apart north south east west
DrivenMadz
Does a RTC chip for say RAK4630 work with meshcore?? (edited)
Uncle Lit 2025-11-02 12:52 a.m.
works for the repeater I used
12:52 a.m.
it kept time for the repeater between power cycles

Uncle Lit 2025-11-02 1:04 a.m.

SeaGL is a grassroots technical conference dedicated to spreading awareness and knowledge about the GNU/Linux community and free/libre/open-source software/hardware.

The Mad Codger 2025-11-10 11:00 p.m.
The major problem—one of the major problems, for there are several—one of the many major problems with governing people is that of whom you get to do it; or rather of who manages to get people to let them do it to them.
To summarize: it is a well-known fact that those people who must want to rule people are, ipso facto, those least suited to do it.
To summarize the summary: anyone who is capable of getting themselves made President should on no account be allowed to do the job.
 (edited)

10:06 p.m.

2:38 p.m.

2:41 p.m.
another tradeoff to consider is that a hell of a lot more people use discord than xmpp or matrix, both protocols have heavily leaned in to bridging to basically "bandaid" that particular tradeoff, but as soon as you start bridging chats to corporate chat systems, you lose a lot of the benefits that you'd otherwise get regarding data privacy

BrianEnigma 2025-11-19 4:53 p.m.
I'm not sure where to ask this or even 100% what I'm asking. I'm in Portland and I can definitely see a lot of conversation happening in the Public and Bot channels that appear to be coming from Seattle. But it seems like my messages aren't making it up there. I can't interact with the HowlBot in the bot channel, for instance. Does it even make sense to have a mesh that only goes one direction? Could I have something misconfigured? Might there be a bad node between here and there? Mostly I'm confused and not sure how I'd even begin troubleshooting pathing.
BrianEnigma
I'm not sure where to ask this or even 100% what I'm asking. I'm in Portland and I can definitely see a lot of conversation happening in the Public and Bot channels that appear to be coming from Seattle. But it seems like my messages aren't making it up there. I can't interact with the HowlBot in the bot channel, for instance. Does it even make sense to have a mesh that only goes one direction? Could I have something misconfigured? Might there be a bad node between here and there? Mostly I'm confused and not sure how I'd even begin troubleshooting pathing.
🌲 Tree 2025-11-19 4:56 p.m.
It's very common for there to be asymmetric connections (i.e., a particular link receives more to a particular neighbor than it sends / is received on the other end). There are probably more radio nerdy people than me to better explain this, but I think atm essentially the connection(s) that are essential for SEA -> PDX are better in that direction than they are in PDX -> SEA
4:58 p.m.
On this page https://analyzer.letsme.sh/channels?region=pnw you can see a string like 1:55 PM - 63 repeats heard by 23 observers [SEA, YVR, OLM] - LP: 5 - Analyze - where SEA was the first IATA this packet was obsered, YVR was the 2nd, and OLM the third*. A packet starting in PDX should always start this list with [PDX, ...] (edited)
BrianEnigma
I'm not sure where to ask this or even 100% what I'm asking. I'm in Portland and I can definitely see a lot of conversation happening in the Public and Bot channels that appear to be coming from Seattle. But it seems like my messages aren't making it up there. I can't interact with the HowlBot in the bot channel, for instance. Does it even make sense to have a mesh that only goes one direction? Could I have something misconfigured? Might there be a bad node between here and there? Mostly I'm confused and not sure how I'd even begin troubleshooting pathing.
Jade 2025-11-19 5:00 p.m.
The messages are coming through 66 - Goat Mountain. When it was originally put up messages would travel both ways, however, something happened probably due to weather where you're only getting them one way now. in the spring, it will be upgraded and also raised higher.. It was also misaligned due to a collision with another repeater with the same prefix. (edited)
5:01 p.m.
Also, some messages do make their way up from Portland but just not a lot (edited)
🌲 Tree 2025-11-19 5:01 p.m.
Again radio nerds will be better with the radio stuff than me - I stick to less magical things, but if you're receiving signals through a node that is very high up, and your node isn't as high or is indoors, I think that will more often lead to these "better rx than tx" scenarios
1
5:03 p.m.
Another thing leading to better rx than tx, is the place you're getting it from could be higher power. Lots of devices are 22dbm and some devices go more than this
Jade 2025-11-19 5:03 p.m.
I suspect at present Portland needs some high power between Portland and goat Mountain with good line of Sight to goat Mountain
2
BrianEnigma 2025-11-19 5:09 p.m.
Thanks everyone! That makes perfect sense. I hadn't considered weather, and I'm kicking myself for not considering asymmetric tx power. It's been years since I've done anything technical with radio, and today mostly focus on [multicast] ethernet packets, hops, and routes.
