1
Uncle Lit 2025-12-17 4:08 p.m.
my opinion is that bridging is not going to work well. the reason being, for a bridge to work, you need to have repeaters at a spot that can see both meshes. you need two repeaters, and some other glue in between. That mean it is a lot harder to run with solar. without solar that means the spots you can have a bridging setup are even selective. If the meshes are bridged successfully and works close to 100% of the time, you will then double each mesh's utilization. If one of the meshes is already very busy, you will start having more and more collisions. This is just me theory crafting. If you see anything wrong with my assumptions, please point them out.
Uncle Lit
my opinion is that bridging is not going to work well. the reason being, for a bridge to work, you need to have repeaters at a spot that can see both meshes. you need two repeaters, and some other glue in between. That mean it is a lot harder to run with solar. without solar that means the spots you can have a bridging setup are even selective. If the meshes are bridged successfully and works close to 100% of the time, you will then double each mesh's utilization. If one of the meshes is already very busy, you will start having more and more collisions. This is just me theory crafting. If you see anything wrong with my assumptions, please point them out.
🌲 Tree 2025-12-17 4:25 p.m.
My opinion is that 1. Bridges will become a necessity 2. Flood traffic will eventually become harshly punished, and directed traffic will be the only thing that works reliably 3. The protocol will need to adapt/change to better support bridging and remain reliable as user counts grow. The mesh can only handle so many people. Adding more repeaters does not increase capacity within an already covered area (in fact, the opposite). Only bridges will. Flood packets, as used by channels and such right now, imo will simply not work if you say for example 10x the user count in the PNW. (edited)
1
1
1
Uncle Lit 2025-12-17 4:27 p.m.
I can see making bridges work only with DMs and drop all flood packets at the bridge
🌲 Tree 2025-12-17 4:27 p.m.
I eventually see many people blocking most flood packets on their repeaters (edited)
4:28 p.m.
Whether it's a bridge or not
4:28 p.m.
Cisien already experimented with this when there was an issue with Home Assistant automation causing tens of thousands of unnecessary packets
4:29 p.m.
I fully expect to see a lot more blocking of flood traffic. Selectively at first, and then very broadly.
4:30 p.m.
In the EU, I read somewhere some users are already blocking several types of flood packets because their airtime util is quite high already
4:30 p.m.
It's inevitable. It's a success problem.
4:32 p.m.
I wish that the automatic routing, DMs, and room servers were the most reliable/awesome part of MeshCore today, but scale/scaling limitations will force it to be eventually
1
1
🌲 Tree
I fully expect to see a lot more blocking of flood traffic. Selectively at first, and then very broadly.
nullrouten 2025-12-17 4:46 p.m.
, we need a way to discover a route (if we’re going to block a Flood) , mesh becomes useless (unless you know exactly which path). Maybe the entire topology can be downloaded offline for companions to ingest. Maybe we can learn from OSPF, EIGRP , BGP, and RIP . Or perhaps the network acts a tiny bit like BitTorrent in disseminating topology for people so they never need to flood. Flood is actually a great way to scale a message to many listeners…. But it’s not really the Flood that’s the problem. It’s people using it to discover where they need to go ….or chatting to the whole public mesh…. If we didn’t need to use Flood, then it wouldn’t be such a problem.
🌲 Tree 2025-12-17 4:52 p.m.
It’s people using it to discover where they need to go ….or chatting to the whole public mesh…
People are trying to build a lot of stuff on flood packets. I've seen people trying to make a BBS on flood channel packets. Or a private channel for people a few miles from each other, but those packets flood 100 miles away. As long as flood packets remain more reliable, they will be the go-to tool. I also don't think blocking flood entirely will happen, but blocking all flood packets except for PATH could realistically happen, and I have observed that in some meshes
nullrouten 2025-12-17 4:53 p.m.

It’s people using it to discover where they need to go ….or chatting to the whole public mesh…
People are trying to build a lot of stuff on flood packets. I've seen people trying to make a BBS on flood channel packets. Or a private channel for people a few miles from each other, but those packets flood 100 miles away. As long as flood packets remain more reliable, they will be the go-to tool. I also don't think blocking flood entirely will happen, but blocking all flood packets except for PATH could realistically happen, and I have observed that in some meshes

5:01 p.m.
it's pretty gross

🌲 Tree 2025-12-17 5:01 p.m.

1
hpux735 2025-12-17 5:01 p.m.
it's only if the packet is already that big. It's not sending 2 packets always just be cause
1
5:02 p.m.
MTU is "Maximum Transfer Unit" not Minimum
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-12-17 5:02 p.m.
right, I haven't looked closer since seeing that
hpux735 2025-12-17 5:02 p.m.
how else are you going to send data that big?
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-12-17 5:02 p.m.
reassembly should almost certainly be done at a higher layer
hpux735 2025-12-17 5:03 p.m.
if you have an ethernet segment with an MTU of 9000, what do you think is happening at a bridge or router?
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-12-17 5:03 p.m.
its done in IP and TCP, not ethernet
hpux735 2025-12-17 5:04 p.m.
that's not true
5:04 p.m.
a bridge is at the MAC layer of the stack. It doesn't know what TCP/IP is
🌲 Tree
If it had idk maybe 10% chance to select the optimal path the first time, every additional time it's still 10% odds of selecting the optimal path. Then the optimal path may still not be 100%, so it could reset and again 10% chance again
Ceres Station 🌑 2025-12-17 5:05 p.m.
i guess it depends on how frequently users leave their "home" repeaters, and how stable and how many repeaters are in between
hpux735
that's not true
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-12-17 5:05 p.m.
it is though
5:06 p.m.
a router that sits across 2 segments with differing MTU's will fragment a large IP packet into smaller IP packets that can be spit out on the lower MTU interface and reassembled by the reciever
hpux735 2025-12-17 5:06 p.m.
I don't feel like arguing about this, but the point is this: If one protocol has an MTU larger than the other, you have to fragment the packets. There is no other way. The only penalty you're paying is the additional packet's header
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-12-17 5:06 p.m.
UDP gets fragmented in the process and that usually breaks the overlying protocol
5:06 p.m.
TCP can gracefully handle the reassembly of single packets at least (edited)
5:07 p.m.
for other non-IP protocols, they'll usually get dropped if you have a packet larger than the MTU of the smaller interface (edited)
5:08 p.m.
ethernet bridges will let packets that are lower than both interfaces MTU through, but it will drop larger frames going to smaller MTU interfaces
hpux735 2025-12-17 5:08 p.m.
you're suggesting rnode should just drop every packet larger than 255?
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-12-17 5:09 p.m.
I'm suggesting that it should be aware of reticulum semantics and that reassembly should be done on a layer up from LoRA
hpux735 2025-12-17 5:11 p.m.
you can always do that if you want with your protocol. But, it sure seems like reticulum was designed before lora was a goal, and the MTU was set at 512. Just throwing up hands and saying you'll never support it seems very silly. Sending two packets is a perfectly reasonable way to handle sending more data. And it would be transparant because rnode would re-assemble, no? If it's not transparently reassembling, it's a bug, IMO
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-12-17 5:11 p.m.
I mean, I just think that the higher MTU requirements make the protocol less than suitable for usage over LoRA
5:11 p.m.
HaLoW or normal WiFi would be fine
hpux735 2025-12-17 5:12 p.m.
but, which came first? You also have to trade-off header overhead. Crypto data tends to be large, and you could end up sending a butt-ton of header data for every packet of real content.
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-12-17 5:12 p.m.
by comparison, ethernet's minimum frame size is 64b
hpux735 2025-12-17 5:13 p.m.
Sure, but every. single. ethernet. frame. isn't encrypted individually (or at all in ethernet)
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-12-17 5:13 p.m.
unless you're doing macsec
hpux735 2025-12-17 5:13 p.m.
look at how much MC packets are made up with crypto. it's a ton
5:13 p.m.
I think it's reasonable to think of ways to have more payload in a packet given the crypto overhead
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-12-17 5:15 p.m.
from a practical standpoint, requiring 2 frames to carry the contents of a single packet doubles the chances of decode failures and data corruption, unless they're being smart about how they're calculating the CRCs
hpux735 2025-12-17 5:16 p.m.
Yah, that part is a bummer. But, it's no different really than if we tried to send a 512 byte packet now. Just look at how much harder it is to send a DM with all 160 characters than it is with a short one.
1
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2025-12-17 5:16 p.m.
if they're telling the radios to enable CRC's, that means there's 2 CRCs that can fail per packet (not including stuff that might be done at a higher layer)
5:16 p.m.
they could turn off crc's and add a single one at the end of both packets, that would help a little bit
hpux735 2025-12-17 5:16 p.m.
in principle, if the chances of corrupting a single bit is x, then it's going to be x^y where why is the number of bits set. Long packets are always problematic
2
5:17 p.m.
Yah, some. But you're always going to run into the probability issue with any long packet on tenuous links like this
5:18 p.m.
that's also where the ability of rns to have an established link is really nice where the crypto secrets are exchanged once and the communication can happen from then on. It really reduces the amount of header data on every packet

Uncle Lit
my opinion is that bridging is not going to work well. the reason being, for a bridge to work, you need to have repeaters at a spot that can see both meshes. you need two repeaters, and some other glue in between. That mean it is a lot harder to run with solar. without solar that means the spots you can have a bridging setup are even selective. If the meshes are bridged successfully and works close to 100% of the time, you will then double each mesh's utilization. If one of the meshes is already very busy, you will start having more and more collisions. This is just me theory crafting. If you see anything wrong with my assumptions, please point them out.
Jef (N5JEF) 2025-12-18 1:30 p.m.
I would be quite willing to engineer a proper bridge between the Sacramento-Foothills and the Bay Area regions. Mount Diablo would be the obvious prime location and I've already reached out some time ago on that possibility... For a bridge to expand our mesh network while maintaining quality will certainly require appropriate filtering of traffic. We know this from sad experience—and solid theory—the solution is not to keep sacrificing link budget for speed. @treehouse ⚡ @Ferret (edited)

mannkind
Also works great for usb companions via ser2net. It’s how I access a companion observer in Castle Rock.
👽 wazmac 2025-12-29 6:53 p.m.
Would you mind sharing your ser2net.yaml for this, as I am having a lot of trouble getting this working for meshcore. TIA
👽 wazmac
Would you mind sharing your ser2net.yaml for this, as I am having a lot of trouble getting this working for meshcore. TIA
mannkind 2025-12-29 7:38 p.m.
Oh, sure. It's nothing super special. connection: &con01 accepter: tcp,20111 connector: serialdev,/dev/serial/by-id/usb-Seeed_Studio_XIAO_nRF52840_<IDENTIFIER>-if00,115200n81,local options: max-connections: 4 The <IDENTIFIER> bit changes depending on the Xiao. I can then run meshcore-packet-capture (companion-based observer for analyzer.letsmesh.net), Home Assistant, and even connect via the app. Though I'll note that via the app you only see messages while connected. (edited)
👽 wazmac 2025-12-29 7:55 p.m.
@mannkind do you think that the messages are only received when connected, as they are being consumed by the other connections as they arrive?
👽 wazmac
@mannkind do you think that the messages are only received when connected, as they are being consumed by the other connections as they arrive?
mannkind 2025-12-29 8:55 p.m.
Yup, exactly.
👽 wazmac 2025-12-29 8:56 p.m.
So if you are connected with the mobile app, you might consume events that that HA is expecting, or do they both get them and process them.
👽 wazmac
So if you are connected with the mobile app, you might consume events that that HA is expecting, or do they both get them and process them.
mannkind 2025-12-29 9:00 p.m.
ser2net broadcasts whatever it’s receiving to all the active connections. So everything gets them. (edited)
👽 wazmac 2025-12-29 9:03 p.m.

👽 wazmac

Symbioquine 2026-01-18 9:39 a.m.
It's surprising to me that there isn't (at least as far as I can tell) some sort of basic throttling/de-duplicating going on... I see two issues related to it, but apparently not much traction:
  • https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/issues/1223
  • https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/issues/1305
It seems obvious that some mechanism to drop spurious/noisy advert repeats will be needed as meshes get very large. Additionally, any protection should be "defensive" - in other words, it should let the network protect itself from (FCC compliant) but misbehaving nodes. (As opposed to relying on good behavior from all nodes.) The next question is: How would that need to work? Issue #1223 explores this a bit, but with the presupposition that it should be probabilistic and without describing the chain of reasoning that leads to that idea. I suspect it is a matter of whether deterministic throttling could be achieved with the small(-ish) memory available on many of the repeater microcontrollers. I'm curious whether that analysis has actually been done and what other strategies have been considered? Some others that I can imagine:
  • Maintain a small local in-memory table of most recent adverts and only repeat those which haven't been heard recently (Simplest and maybe avoids at least the worst spam cases)
  • Replace certain key repeaters with fancier hardware (like Raspberry Pis) that have enough memory to track multiple days of all the adverts heard and - like above - only repeat those which meet the throttling criteria (e.g. greater than 24h)
  • Implement a gossip protocol that utilizes idle radio time to build a distributed list of the noisiest nodes so that cooperating repeaters can consistently rate throttle adverts (and even other messages) from those nodes.
  • (Probably lots of other strategies that's just off the top of my head...)
Cisien 2026-01-18 11:01 a.m.
Not much cougar, haystack, and similar nodes can do when node are adverting too frequently as they see many of them directly. There is basic dedupe logic based on the payload hash
1

11:58 a.m.

11:59 a.m.

12:04 p.m.

🌲 Tree 2026-01-19 11:39 p.m.

11:41 p.m.

11:42 p.m.

1
[PNW] HerculesMulligan 2026-01-19 11:52 p.m.
Dan over on the Cascadia mesh proposed using one of the reserved prefixes (FF) in his example, as a way to say "repeat me this many times". You send a path of FFFFFFFF and the first repeater makes 01FFFFFF, then 017AFFFF, etc. until exhausted.
Dean 2026-01-20 2:36 a.m.
Is there any meshcore activities / repeaters in the Seaside, OR area ? Will be there for SeaPac 2026.
[PNW] HerculesMulligan
Dan over on the Cascadia mesh proposed using one of the reserved prefixes (FF) in his example, as a way to say "repeat me this many times". You send a path of FFFFFFFF and the first repeater makes 01FFFFFF, then 017AFFFF, etc. until exhausted.
Symbioquine 2026-01-20 7:50 a.m.
Hop counts/ttl-sorts-of-things only solve the scaling problem if the mesh graph isn't very dense...
🌲 Tree
Ultimately that would not solve the finite airtime problem, and as utilization continued to grow (even without adverts), eventually the odds of an advert being successful drops to the point where that once every 2 week checkin may not get repeated at all, or very broadly.
Symbioquine 2026-01-20 8:03 a.m.
If the data in adverts is useful and going to solve for a real mesh user story (other than "this is cool to have an automatic map/contact-list"), I believe the mesh software would need to be smarter about how to propagate that information - probably a gossip protocol that uses lower airtime moments to share information about what nodes are online and how recently they've been seen. Such a protocol could be pretty efficient airtime-wise because most of the information in adverts doesn't need to be repeated every time and the "fidelity of the information communicated could be spatially attenuated" (I'll try to explain what I mean by this in the next message). In my mind only the stable portions of the mesh - repeaters/rooms - participate in that gossip protocol. Companions then query their nearby mesh to get the information they need.
8:08 a.m.
What I mean by "fidelity of [mesh] information being spatially attenuated" is that a user in Bellingham, WA probably doesn't care about the exact locations and to-the-minute uptimes of the the Portland, OR repeaters. Just some basic node counts and maybe buckets of uptime - e.g. "300 fixed nodes seen in the last 24h and 363 fixed nodes seen in the last 48h". So, far away node information can be communicated with fewer digits of precision. (edited)
8:14 a.m.
"most of the information in adverts doesn't need to be repeated every time"
Probably this is obvious, but with the gossip protocol strategy, sending a 32bit pubkey and 64bit signature with every nodes' information is hugely wasteful. The pubkey prefix can be truncated dynamically so it is as short as possible without being ambiguous and many nodes information can be communicated within a single signed communication "stream".
8:15 a.m.
sending a 32bit pubkey and 64bit signature with every nodes' information is hugely wasteful
This is of course not a criticism of the existing protocol. It is a very reasonable starting point for prototyping/bootstrapping a mesh network protocol.
1
🌲 Tree
I think something that would maybe help is a different max hop setting/build flag in the firmware for flood adverts. Maybe max hops is 64, but for adverts, it's capped at idk 8 or 32 or something less

hpux735 2026-01-28 5:41 p.m.

Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2026-01-28 5:48 p.m.
yeah, big endian is also known as network endian for a reason (edited)
1
hpux735 2026-01-28 6:07 p.m.
Oh, there's another (2) things that's so so frustrating... There's no payload length field for encrypted packets, and it uses a block cipher, so every packet payload is a multiple of 16 bytes, and you have no idea how long the content is.
6:08 p.m.
the other is that the response packet has no field to indicate what type it is, you have to know what the request was. Stateful protocols aren't really great.
1
ded (SalishMesh)

mcarper 2026-01-29 5:52 p.m.
At the protocol level if only ascii char is used; change the encoding from 8bits per char to 7bits or 6bits or 5bits (lowercase with some puncuation) 4bits (hex only). Would make most chats take up less airtime and not use typical compression; just changing how many bits you need to print a character. Default would be utf8 which offers everything currently. Sharing a public key; 4 bits typing a simple message with no numbers or uppercase: 5bits Full uppercase and lowercase and numbers: 6bit All the odd ASCII characters: 7bit Full chat with emojis: utf8
5:54 p.m.
This bits used for encoding would be done client side

it'd be utf8 limits

alj 2026-04-18 12:52 p.m.
I see that majority of repeaters which are switching to multibyte hash have picked 3 byte hash. But i cannot find any discussion around it. Is it just a coincidence or there is some rationale for picking 3 byte vs 2 byte?
ded (SalishMesh) 2026-04-18 1:09 p.m.
More precise essentially
1:09 p.m.
Some mesh are very large and even at 2 bytes conflicts are common (edited)
alj
I see that majority of repeaters which are switching to multibyte hash have picked 3 byte hash. But i cannot find any discussion around it. Is it just a coincidence or there is some rationale for picking 3 byte vs 2 byte?
Cisien 2026-04-18 1:32 p.m.
Bigger number better… its a personal choice that doesn’t impact the mesh. The only downside about a 3 byte prefix on a repeater is about 10 less max hops for that repeater’s advert. Which is of limited value (edited)
alj 2026-04-18 1:33 p.m.
It seems like 3 byte trace still reaches British Columbia just fine.
Cisien 2026-04-18 1:34 p.m.
Message delivery chance drops fast after about 10 hops, so limiting to 22 with 3 byte prefixes isn’t much if a downside
1
1:35 p.m.
Cisien
Bigger number better… its a personal choice that doesn’t impact the mesh. The only downside about a 3 byte prefix on a repeater is about 10 less max hops for that repeater’s advert. Which is of limited value (edited)
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2026-04-18 2:02 p.m.
it impacts the mesh in the sense that packet paths grow in size 2-3x faster which results in bigger packets and more airtime usage. both on flooded packets (where repeaters append their ID), and on directs
Cisien 2026-04-18 2:31 p.m.
sure
Dan - WI7FI [PNW]
it impacts the mesh in the sense that packet paths grow in size 2-3x faster which results in bigger packets and more airtime usage. both on flooded packets (where repeaters append their ID), and on directs
alj 2026-04-18 2:46 p.m.
But also 3 byte hash caps numbe of hops decreasing network load potentially? It is probably a real issue if majority nodes have dupe detection off and avg number of hops is 15.6
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2026-04-18 2:47 p.m.
potentially
2:47 p.m.
i think it's going to increase the average packet size though
Cisien 2026-04-18 3:14 p.m.
repeats are the biggest problem for most well-placed repeaters
3:15 p.m.
1 for 1, haystack would have 6% rx airtime to match its 6% tx airtime (edited)
3:15 p.m.
but since it sees everything it can't decode everything it receives because it's just too much
3:17 p.m.
we can bicker about 10 extra bytes of airtime, but unless we take steps to reduce flood traffic, it's not going to make a difference. and flood traffic is integral to the protocol as it's used by the vast majority.
Dan - WI7FI [PNW] 2026-04-18 3:22 p.m.
pretty sure usage of region keys will help dramatically
Cisien 2026-04-18 3:24 p.m.
no, it won't really help because those packets are still transmitted. The local retransmit is the most impactful, not packets routed in from distant sites
3:24 p.m.
and i'm certain that we won't see broad adoption of regions, just a split into the nodes that use them, and nodes that don't
3:25 p.m.
not without it being a firmware default somehow
alj 2026-04-18 4:20 p.m.
Please see if this logic is correct: 1. for repeaters 3 bytes might be the way to go, they don't flood often (hopefully!) but you don't want any even low probability collisions to stand in the way of connecting to your repeater for management purposes. 2. for companion devices 2 byte may be the way to go because in-transit hash size depends on a sender settings, i e "3 byte" repeater will append 2 byte path if sender had that in settings.... (edited)
Cisien
Click to see attachment 🖼️
Uncle Lit 2026-04-19 11:28 a.m.
If this math is correct, this table is only true forDMs that are sent out with a set path. Flood messages have multiple paths and therefore multiple chances to go further,
Uncle Lit
If this math is correct, this table is only true forDMs that are sent out with a set path. Flood messages have multiple paths and therefore multiple chances to go further,

alj 2026-04-19 3:49 p.m.

ded (SalishMesh) 2026-04-19 3:57 p.m.

3:58 p.m.

3:58 p.m.

ded (SalishMesh)
Use whatever region is actually in use in your area
alj 2026-04-20 12:40 a.m.
I think (also based on example-repeater code) when you set default region and that region is absent in a region map of a repeater (which is how 99.99% of all repeaters configured now) it does not fall back to a wildcard region. Which is a logical bug. Because if that fallback does not happen, you cannot seamlessly transition to region maps.
